'''岡山県護国神社'''（おかやまけんごこくじんじゃ）とは[[岡山県]][[岡山市]]奥市にある[[護国神社]]である。境内には戦死者の慰霊碑が数多く建立されており、[[シベリア抑留]]者や[[宇垣纒]]提督と十七勇士の菊水慰霊碑などがある。
== 沿革 ==
岡山藩主であった[[池田章政]]が、[[1869年]][[4月3日]]に現在の[[後楽園]]公園裏竹田河原に於て、[[奥羽戦争]]戦死者である雀部八郎時宜など34柱の招魂祭を挙行し、6月に上道郡門田村幣立山地180坪に社殿と碑石を建立し、奥羽、函館両戦争戦死者を祭祀したのが起源である。
[[1874年]][[3月17日]]に官祭となり、[[1922年]]に現在地に移転し4月26日に社殿竣工した。1939年4月1日に内務大臣指定と共に「岡山縣護國神社」と改称した。戦後の一時期は[[連合国]]の占領政策により「操山神社」としていたが[[1952年]]に現在の名称に復帰した。

'''岡山県護国神社'''（おかやまけんごこくじんじゃ）とは[[岡山県]][[岡山市]]奥市にある[[護国神社]]である。境内には戦死者の慰霊碑が数多く建立されており、[[シベリア抑留]]者や[[宇垣纒]]提督と十七勇士の菊水慰霊碑などがある。
== 沿革 ==
岡山藩主であった[[池田章政]]が、[[1869年]][[4月3日]]に現在の[[後楽園]]公園裏竹田河原に於て、[[奥羽戦争]]戦死者である雀部八郎時宜など34柱の招魂祭を挙行し、6月に上道郡門田村幣立山地180坪に社殿と碑石を建立し、奥羽、函館両戦争戦死者を祭祀したのが起源である。
[[1874年]][[3月17日]]に官祭となり、岡山招魂社となった。[[1922年]]に現在地に移転し4月26日に社殿竣工した。1939年4月1日に内務大臣指定と共に「岡山縣護國神社」と改称した。戦後の一時期は[[連合国]]の占領政策により「操山神社」としていたが[[1952年]]に現在の名称に復帰した。また戦後になり改修工事などが行われている。

境内には戦死者の慰霊碑が数多く建立されており、[[シベリア抑留]]者や[[宇垣纒]]提督と十七勇士の菊水慰霊碑などがある。
== 沿革 ==
岡山藩主であった[[池田章政]]が、[[1869年]](明治2年)4月3日に現在の[[後楽園]]公園裏竹田河原に於て、[[奥羽戦争]]戦死者である雀部八郎時宜など34柱の招魂祭を挙行し、6月に上道郡門田村幣立山地180坪に社殿と碑石を建立し、奥羽、函館両戦争戦死者を祭祀したのが起源である。
[[1874年]](明治7年)3月17日に官祭となり、岡山[[招魂社]]となった。[[1922年]](大正4年)に現在地に移転、4月26日に社殿が竣工した。[[1939年]](昭和14年)4月1日に内務大臣指定と共に「岡山縣護國神社」と改称した。戦後の一時期は[[連合国]]の占領政策により「操山神社」としていたが[[1952年]](昭和27年)に現在の名称に復帰した。また戦後になり改修工事などが行われている。

{{神社| 名称=岡山県護国神社| 所在地=岡山県岡山市中区奥市3-21| 位置={{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|34|39|26.6|N|133|57|19.4|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=岡山県護国神社}}| 祭神=岡山県出身並に縁故ある殉国の英霊| 社格=内務大臣指定護国神社・別表神社| 創建=1869年（明治2年）| 例祭=| 画像=[[File:Okayamaken Gokoku Jinja 04.JPG|250px]]}} '''岡山県護国神社'''（おかやまけんごこくじんじゃ）とは[[岡山県]][[岡山市]][[中区 (岡山市)|中区]]奥市にある[[護国神社]]である。旧[[近代社格制度|社格]]は内務大臣指定護国神社で、戦後[[別表神社]]となった。
境内には戦死者の慰霊碑が数多く建立されており、[[シベリア抑留]]者や[[宇垣纒]]海軍中将と十七勇士の菊水慰霊碑などがある。
== 沿革 ==
岡山藩主であった[[池田章政]]が、[[1869年]]（[[明治]]2年）4月3日に現在の[[後楽園]]公園裏竹田河原に於て、[[戊辰戦争|奥羽戦争]]戦死者である雀部八郎時宜など34柱の招魂祭を挙行し、6月に上道郡門田村幣立山地180坪に社殿と碑石を建立し、奥羽、函館両戦争戦死者を祭祀したのが起源である。
[[1874年]]（明治7年）3月17日に官祭となり、岡山[[招魂社]]となった。[[1922年]]（大正4年）に現在地に移転、4月26日に社殿が竣工した。[[1939年]]（昭和14年）4月1日に内務大臣指定と共に「岡山縣護國神社」と改称した。戦後の一時期は[[連合国 (第二次世界大戦)|連合国]]の占領政策により「操山神社」としていたが[[1952年]]（昭和27年）に現在の名称に復帰した。また戦後になり改修工事などが行われている。
== アクセス == *[[岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線]] *[[両備ホールディングス|両備バス]][[西大寺バスセンター|西大寺]]行きに乗車、「護国神社」下車

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=2017-8-28}}{{Shrine | name={{ill|Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine|ja|岡山県護国神社}} | location={{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka-ku|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} Okuichi 3-21 | coordinates={{Coord|34|39|26.6|N|133|57|19.4|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=岡山県護国神社}} | deity={{ill|Spirits of the war dead from Okayama Prefecture and those with connections|ja|岡山県出身並に縁故ある殉国の英霊}} | shakaku={{ill|Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs|ja|内務大臣指定護国神社}}・{{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} | established={{ill|1869|ja|1869年}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2年) | main_festival= | image=[[File:Okayamaken Gokoku Jinja 04.JPG|250px]] }}
'''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} located in Okuichi, {{ill|Naka-ku|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. The former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a {{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} after the war. [[Ja:岡山護国神社]] The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.
== History == {{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政}}, the lord of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜}} from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争}}, on April 3, {{ill|1869|ja|1869年}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2), at the current site of {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}} park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争}}.
On March 17, [[:en:1874|1874]] (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In [[:en:1922|1922]] (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, {{ill|1939|ja|1939年}} (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in {{ill|:en:1952|ja|1952年|lt=1955}} (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.
== Access == * {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線}} * Take the {{ill|Ryobi Bus|ja|両備ホールディングス}} bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.
== External Links == {{commons-inline|Category:Okayamaken-Gokoku-jinja}}
{{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかやまけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Okayama]] [[Category:History of Okayama]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naka-ku, Okayama]] [[Category: [[Category:Shinto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine[[Category:Shinto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Shinto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine[[Category:Shinto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:original pages]] [[Category:Shinto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:original pages]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine[[Category:Shinto]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine}}[[Category:original pages]] ]]

| location={{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka-ku|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} Okuichi 3-21 | coordinates={{Coord|34|39|26.6|N|133|57|19.4|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=岡山県護国神社}} | deity={{ill|Spirits of the war dead from Okayama Prefecture and those with connections|ja|岡山県出身並に縁故ある殉国の英霊}} | shakaku={{ill|Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs|ja|内務大臣指定護国神社}}・{{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} | established={{ill|1869|ja|1869年}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2年) | main_festival= | image=[[File:Okayamaken Gokoku Jinja 04.JPG|250px]] }}
'''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} located in Okuichi, {{ill|Naka-ku|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. The former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a {{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} after the war. [[Ja:岡山護国神社]] The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.
== History == {{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政}}, the lord of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜}} from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争}}, on April 3, {{ill|1869|ja|1869年}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2), at the current site of {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}} park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争}}.
On March 17, [[:en:1874|1874]] (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In [[:en:1922|1922]] (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, {{ill|1939|ja|1939年}} (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in {{ill|:en:1952|ja|1952年|lt=1955}} (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.
== Access == * {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線}} * Take the {{ill|Ryobi Bus|ja|両備ホールディングス}} bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.
== External Links == {{commons-inline|Category:Pages that were the oldest for a long time so I am bumping them up the queue for this round]][[Category:Okayamaken-Gokoku-jinja}}
{{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おかやまけんここくしんしや}} [[Category:Shinto shrines in Okayama]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Okayama]] [[Category:History of Okayama]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Gokoku shrines]]{{Gokoku Shrines}}[[Category:Gokoku shrines]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Naka-ku, Okayama]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Okayama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Okayama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Okayama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Okayama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Okayama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Okayama Gokoku Shrine}} [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Okayama Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]]

{{Unreferenced|date=2017-8-28}}{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name=Okayama Gokoku Shrine
| location={{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka-ku|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} Okuichi 3-21 | coordinates={{Coord|34|39|26.6|N|133|57|19.4|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=岡山県護国神社}} | deity={{ill|Spirits of the war dead from Okayama Prefecture and those with connections|ja|岡山県出身並に縁故ある殉国の英霊}} | shakaku={{ill|Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs|ja|内務大臣指定護国神社}}・{{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} | established={{ill|1869|ja|1869年}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2年) | main_festival= | image=[[File:Okayamaken Gokoku Jinja 04.JPG|250px]] }}
'''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} located in Okuichi, {{ill|Naka-ku|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. The former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a {{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} after the war. [[Ja:岡山護国神社]] The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.
== History == {{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政}}, the lord of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜}} from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争}}, on April 3, {{ill|1869|ja|1869年}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2), at the current site of {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}} park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争}}.
On March 17, [[:en:1874|1874]] (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In [[:en:1922|1922]] (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, {{ill|1939|ja|1939年}} (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in {{ill|:en:1952|ja|1952年|lt=1955}} (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.
== Access == * {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線}} * Take the {{ill|Ryobi Bus|ja|両備ホールディングス}} bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.

'''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} located in Okuichi, {{ill|Naka-ku|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. The former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a {{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} after the war. [[Ja:岡山護国神社]] The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.
== History == {{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政}}, the lord of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜}} from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争}}, on April 3, {{ill|1869|ja|1869年}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2), at the current site of {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}} park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争}}.
On March 17, [[:en:1874|1874]] (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In [[:en:1922|1922]] (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, {{ill|1939|ja|1939年}} (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in {{ill|1952|ja|1952年|lt=1955}} (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.
== Access == * {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線}} * Take the {{ill|Ryobi Bus|ja|両備ホールディングス}} bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.

{{Unreferenced|date=2017-8-28}}{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name=Okayama Gokoku Shrine
| location={{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka-ku|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} Okuichi 3-21 | coordinates={{Coord|34|39|26.6|N|133|57|19.4|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=岡山県護国神社}} | deity={{ill|Spirits of the war dead from Okayama Prefecture and those with connections|ja|岡山県出身並に縁故ある殉国の英霊}} | shakaku={{ill|Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs|ja|内務大臣指定護国神社}}・{{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} | established={{ill|1869|ja|1869年}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2年) | main_festival= | image=[[File:Okayamaken Gokoku Jinja 04.JPG|250px]] }}
'''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} located in Okuichi, {{ill|Naka-ku|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. The former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a {{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} after the war. [[Ja:岡山護国神社]] The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.
== History == {{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政}}, the lord of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜}} from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争}}, on April 3, {{ill|1869|ja|1869年}} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2), at the current site of {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}} park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争}}.
On March 17, [[:en:1874|1874]] (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In [[:en:1922|1922]] (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, {{ill|1939|ja|1939年}} (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in {{ill|1952|ja|1952年|lt=1955}} (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.
== Access == * {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線}} * Take the {{ill|Ryobi Bus|ja|両備ホールディングス}} bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.

revision.text:
{{Unreferenced|date=2017-8-28}}{{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name=Okayama Gokoku Shrine
| location={{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}{{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}{{ill|Naka-ku|ja|中区 (岡山市)}} Okuichi 3-21 | coordinates={{Coord|34|39|26.6|N|133|57|19.4|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=岡山県護国神社}} | deity={{ill|Spirits of the war dead from Okayama Prefecture and those with connections|ja|岡山県出身並に縁故ある殉国の英霊}} | shakaku={{ill|Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs|ja|内務大臣指定護国神社}}・{{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} | established={{ill|1869|ja|1869th year }} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2nd year ) | main_festival= | image=[[File:Okayamaken Gokoku Jinja 04.JPG|250px]] }}
'''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} located in Okuichi, {{ill|Naka-ku|ja|中区 (岡山市)}}, {{ill|Okayama|ja|岡山市}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|ja|岡山県}}. The former [[Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a {{ill|Beppyo shrine|ja|別表神社}} after the war. [[Ja:岡山護国神社]] The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.
== History == {{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政}}, the lord of {{ill|Okayama Domain|ja|岡山藩}}, held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜}} from the {{ill|Boshin War|ja|戊辰戦争}}'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争}}, on April 3, {{ill|1869|ja|1869th year }} ({{ill|Meiji|ja|明治}} 2), at the current site of {{ill|Korakuen|ja|後楽園}} park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争}}.
On March 17, [[:en:1874|1874]] (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In [[:en:1922|1922]] (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, {{ill|1939|ja|1939th year }} (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in {{ill|1952|ja|1952nd year |lt=1955}} (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.
== Access == * {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線}} * Take the {{ill|Ryobi Bus|ja|両備ホールディングス}} bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.
== External Links == {{commons-inline|Category:Okayamaken-Gokoku-jinja}}

'''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in Okuichi, [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka-ku]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. The former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after the war.
[[Ja:岡山護国神社]]
The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matome Ugaki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇垣纏|1=Matome Ugaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1148126}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.

== History ==
{{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda Noriyuki|qq=|1=Akimasa Ikeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11552088}}, the lord of [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Aizu|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戊辰戦争|1=Boshin War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q309184}}, on April 3, [[:en:1869|1869]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 2), at the current site of [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]] park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate War|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱館戦争|1=Battle of Hakodate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q716562}}.

On March 17, [[:en:1874|1874]] (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In [[:en:1922|1922]] (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, [[:en:1939|1939]] (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in [[:en:1952|1955]] (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.

== Access ==
* {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|qq=|1=Okayama Prefectural Road Route 28|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11472488}}
* Take the [[:en:Ryobi Holdings|Ryobi Bus]] bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saidaiji Bus Center|qq=|1=Saidaiji Bus Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627554}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.

'''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in Okuichi, [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka-ku]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. The former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after the war.
[[Ja:岡山護国神社]]
The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matome Ugaki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇垣纏|1=Matome Ugaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1148126}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.

== History ==
{{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda Noriyuki|1=Akimasa Ikeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11552088}}, the lord of [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Aizu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戊辰戦争|1=Boshin War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q309184}}, on April 3, [[:en:1869|1869]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 2), at the current site of [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]] park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate War|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱館戦争|1=Battle of Hakodate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q716562}}.

On March 17, [[:en:1874|1874]] (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In [[:en:1922|1922]] (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, [[:en:1939|1939]] (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in [[:en:1952|1955]] (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.

== Access ==
* {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|1=Okayama Prefectural Road Route 28|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11472488}}
* Take the [[:en:Ryobi Holdings|Ryobi Bus]] bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saidaiji Bus Center|1=Saidaiji Bus Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627554}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.

 | coordinates={{Coord|34|39|26.6|N|133|57|19.4|E|region:JP-33_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=岡山県護国神社}}
 | deity={{ill|Spirits of the war dead from Okayama Prefecture and those with connections|ja|岡山県出身並に縁故ある殉国の英霊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spirits of the war dead from Okayama Prefecture and those with connections|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

 | main_festival=
 | image=[[File:Okayamaken Gokoku Jinja 04.JPG|250px]]
}}

'''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in Okuichi, [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka-ku]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. The former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after the war.
[[Ja:岡山護国神社]]
The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matome Ugaki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇垣纏|1=Matome Ugaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1148126}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.

== History ==
{{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda Noriyuki|1=Akimasa Ikeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11552088}}, the lord of [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Aizu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戊辰戦争|1=Boshin War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q309184}}, on April 3, [[:en:1869|1869]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 2), at the current site of [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]] park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate War|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱館戦争|1=Battle of Hakodate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q716562}}.

On March 17, [[:en:1874|1874]] (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In [[:en:1922|1922]] (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, [[:en:1939|1939]] (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in [[:en:1952|1955]] (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.

== Access ==
* {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|1=Okayama Prefectural Road Route 28|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11472488}}
* Take the [[:en:Ryobi Holdings|Ryobi Bus]] bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saidaiji Bus Center|1=Saidaiji Bus Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627554}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.

'''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in Okuichi, [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka-ku]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. The former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after the war.
[[Ja:岡山護国神社]]
The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matome Ugaki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇垣纏|1=Matome Ugaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1148126}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.

== History ==
{{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda Noriyuki|1=Akimasa Ikeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11552088}}, the lord of [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Aizu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戊辰戦争|1=Boshin War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q309184}}, on April 3, [[:en:1869|1869]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 2), at the current site of [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]] park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate War|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱館戦争|1=Battle of Hakodate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q716562}}.

On March 17, [[:en:1874|1874]] (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In [[:en:1922|1922]] (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, [[:en:1939|1939]] (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in [[:en:1952|1955]] (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.

== Access ==
* {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|1=Okayama Prefectural Road Route 28|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11472488}}
* Take the [[:en:Ryobi Holdings|Ryobi Bus]] bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saidaiji Bus Center|1=Saidaiji Bus Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627554}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.

For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in [[:en:1952|1955]] (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.

'''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in Okuichi, [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka-ku]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. The former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after the war.
[[Ja:岡山護国神社]]
The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matome Ugaki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇垣纏|1=Matome Ugaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1148126}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.

== History ==
{{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda Noriyuki|1=Akimasa Ikeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11552088}}, the lord of [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Aizu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戊辰戦争|1=Boshin War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q309184}}, on April 3, [[:en:1869|1869]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 2), at the current site of [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]] park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate War|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱館戦争|1=Battle of Hakodate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q716562}}.

On March 17, [[:en:1874|1874]] (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In [[:en:1922|1922]] (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, [[:en:1939|1939]] (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in [[:en:1952|1955]] (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.

== Access ==
* {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|1=Okayama Prefectural Road Route 28|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11472488}}
* Take the [[:en:Ryobi Holdings|Ryobi Bus]] bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saidaiji Bus Center|1=Saidaiji Bus Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627554}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.

The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matome Ugaki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇垣纏|1=Matome Ugaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1148126}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.

== History ==
{{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda Noriyuki|1=Akimasa Ikeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11552088}}, the lord of [[:en:Okayama Domain|Okayama Domain]], held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from the [[:en:Boshin War|Boshin War]]'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Aizu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戊辰戦争|1=Boshin War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q309184}}, on April 3, [[:en:1869|1869]] ([[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]] 2), at the current site of [[:en:Kōraku-en|Korakuen]] park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate War|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱館戦争|1=Battle of Hakodate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q716562}}.

On March 17, [[:en:1874|1874]] (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In [[:en:1922|1922]] (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, [[:en:1939|1939]] (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in [[:en:1952|1955]] (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.

== Access ==
* {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|1=Okayama Prefectural Road Route 28|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11472488}}
* Take the [[:en:Ryobi Holdings|Ryobi Bus]] bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saidaiji Bus Center|1=Saidaiji Bus Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627554}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.

'''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a [[:en:Gokoku shrines|Gokoku Shrine]] located in Okuichi, [[:en:Naka-ku, Okayama|Naka-ku]], [[:en:Okayama|Okayama]], [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]. The former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a [[:en:Beppyo shrine|Beppyo Shrine]] after the war.

On March 17, [[:en:1874|1874]] (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In [[:en:1922|1922]] (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, [[:en:1939|1939]] (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in [[:en:1952|1955]] (Showa 27). '''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine}} located in Okuichi, {{ill|Naka-ku, Okayama|en|Naka-ku, Okayama|WD=Q852116|lt=Naka-ku}}, {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}. The former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|WD=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine}} after the war.
[[Ja:岡山護国神社]]
The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matome Ugaki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇垣纏|1=Matome Ugaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1148126}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.

== History ==
{{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda Noriyuki|1=Akimasa Ikeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11552088}}, the lord of {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|Okayama Domain|WD=Q903497|lt=Okayama Domain}}, held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War}}'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Aizu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戊辰戦争|1=Boshin War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q309184}}, on April 3, {{ill|1869|en|1869|WD=Q7720|lt=1869}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era}} 2), at the current site of {{ill|Kōraku-en|en|Kōraku-en|WD=Q1377689|lt=Korakuen}} park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate War|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱館戦争|1=Battle of Hakodate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q716562}}.

On March 17, {{ill|1874|en|1874|WD=Q7761|lt=1874}} (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In {{ill|1922|en|1922|WD=Q2165|lt=1922}} (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, {{ill|1939|en|1939|WD=Q18639|lt=1939}} (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in {{ill|1952|en|1952|WD=Q5272|lt=1955}} (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.

== Access ==
* {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|1=Okayama Prefectural Road Route 28|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11472488}}
* Take the {{ill|Ryobi Holdings|en|Ryobi Holdings|WD=Q11362596|lt=Ryobi Bus}} bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saidaiji Bus Center|1=Saidaiji Bus Center|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627554}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.

'''Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine''' (岡山護国神社) is a {{ill|Gokoku shrines|en|Gokoku shrines|WD=Q1534477|lt=Gokoku Shrine|1=Gokoku Shrines}} located in Okuichi, {{ill|Naka-ku, Okayama|en|Naka-ku, Okayama|WD=Q852116|lt=Naka-ku|1=Naka-ku, Okayama}}, {{ill|Okayama|en|Okayama|WD=Q200078|lt=Okayama|1=Okayama}}, {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture|1=Okayama Prefecture}}. The former [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines]] classification was a Designated Gokoku Shrine by the Minister of Home Affairs, and it became a {{ill|Beppyo shrine|en|Beppyo shrine|WD=Q10898274|lt=Beppyo Shrine|1=Beppyo shrine}} after the war.
[[Ja:岡山護国神社]]
The shrine grounds feature numerous memorial monuments to the war dead, including memorials for those interned in Siberia and a memorial for Navy Vice Admiral {{ill|Matome Ugaki|ja|宇垣纒|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matome Ugaki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宇垣纏|1=Matome Ugaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1148126}} and the seventeen warriors of Kikusui.

== History ==
{{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda Noriyuki|1=Akimasa Ikeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11552088}}, the lord of {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|Okayama Domain|WD=Q903497|lt=Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain}}, held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}}'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Aizu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戊辰戦争|1=Boshin War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q309184}}, on April 3, {{ill|1869|en|1869|WD=Q7720|lt=1869|1=1869}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 2), at the current site of {{ill|Kōraku-en|en|Kōraku-en|WD=Q1377689|lt=Korakuen|1=Kōraku-en}} park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate War|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱館戦争|1=Battle of Hakodate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q716562}}.

On March 17, {{ill|1874|en|1874|WD=Q7761|lt=1874|1=1874}} (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In {{ill|1922|en|1922|WD=Q2165|lt=1922|1=1922}} (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, {{ill|1939|en|1939|WD=Q18639|lt=1939|1=1939}} (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in {{ill|1952|en|1952|WD=Q5272|lt=1955|1=1952}} (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.

== Access ==
* {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|1=Okayama Prefectural Road Route 28|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11472488}}
* Take the {{ill|Ryobi Holdings|en|Ryobi Holdings|WD=Q11362596|lt=Ryobi Bus|1=Ryobi Holdings}} bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saidaiji Bus Center|1=Q11627554|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627554}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.

{{ill|Ikeda Noriyuki|ja|池田章政|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikeda Noriyuki|1=Akimasa Ikeda|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11552088}}, the lord of {{ill|Okayama Domain|en|Okayama Domain|WD=Q903497|lt=Okayama Domain|1=Okayama Domain}}, held a spirit summoning festival for 34 war dead, including {{ill|Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja|雀部八郎時宜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Suzume Hachiro Tokioki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}}'s {{ill|Battle of Aizu|ja|奥羽戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Battle of Aizu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 戊辰戦争|1=Boshin War|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q309184}}, on April 3, {{ill|1869|en|1869|WD=Q7720|lt=1869|1=1869}} ({{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|WD=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} 2), at the current site of {{ill|Kōraku-en|en|Kōraku-en|WD=Q1377689|lt=Korakuen|1=Kōraku-en}} park's back bamboo field. In June of the same year, a shrine and stone monument were built on an 180 tsubo site in Kamida Village, Joto District, to worship the war dead from the Aizu and {{ill|Hakodate War|ja|函館戦争|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakodate War|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 箱館戦争|1=Battle of Hakodate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q716562}}.

On March 17, {{ill|1874|en|1874|WD=Q7761|lt=1874|1=1874}} (Meiji 7), it became an official government shrine and was named Okayama Shokonsha. In {{ill|1922|en|1922|WD=Q2165|lt=1922|1=1922}} (Taisho 4), it was relocated to its current location, and the shrine buildings were completed on April 26. On April 1, {{ill|1939|en|1939|WD=Q18639|lt=1939|1=1939}} (Showa 14), it was designated by the Minister of Home Affairs and renamed Okayama Prefecture Gokoku Shrine. For a period after the war, it was called Sōzan Shrine due to the Allied occupation policies, but it reverted to its current name in {{ill|1952|en|1952|WD=Q5272|lt=1955|1=1952}} (Showa 27). Various renovations have been carried out since the war.

== Access ==
* {{ill|Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|ja|岡山県道28号岡山牛窓線|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okayama Prefectural Route 28 Okayama Ushimado Line|1=Okayama Prefectural Road Route 28|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11472488}}
* Take the {{ill|Ryobi Holdings|en|Ryobi Holdings|WD=Q11362596|lt=Ryobi Bus|1=Ryobi Holdings}} bound for {{ill|Saidaiji Bus Center|ja|西大寺バスセンター|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saidaiji Bus Center|1=Q11627554|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11627554}} and get off at "Gokoku Shrine" bus stop.
